title: 1.1 Whole-genome sequencing - What & Why
text: Whole-genome sequencing (WGS) is a laboratory technique used to determine the complete DNA sequence of an organism’s genome. Unlike traditional methods that target specific genes, WGS sequences every part of the genome, providing a comprehensive view of the organism’s genetic makeup.

title: 1.1 Whole-genome sequencing - What & Why
section: Klebsiella pneumoniae Outbreak in an Italian Hospital
text: Klebsiella pneumoniae Outbreak in an Italian Hospital

NCBI Resources: NCBI

title: 3.1 Read mapping & SNP alignment

title: Day 3: Phylogenetics - Relationships between genomes
text:
At this stage, we’re gonna learn how to compare and infer the evolutionary relationships between the outbreak’s isolates from their short-read sequencing data. These analyses will enable us to answer questions, such as:

Are the outbreak isolates related to each other?
How many transmission events occurred during the outbreak?
What is the direction of transmission?
How does the outbreak spread in the hospital?
How does the pathogen evolve during the outbreak?
What could be the origin of the outbreak?

We will journey through the following topics:

Genome mapping: How to map sequencing reads of the outbreak’s isolates to a reference genome.
SNP calling & alignment: How to identify single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) among the outbreak’s isolates.
Phylogenetic tree inference: How to infer evolutionary relationships between the outbreak’s isolates.

Now, let’s begin!

title: Day 0: Pack your backpack
text:
Welcome to the MicroGen 2026 workshop! This workshop is designed to equip you with the essential computational skills needed for microbial genomics and bioinformatics. Over the next four days, we will journey from the basics of the Linux computing environment to advanced phylogenetic analysis and metagenomics, while also applying our learning to real-world study cases in infectious diseases.



Day 0: Setting up your computational environment and getting comfortable with the Linux environment and command lines.
Day 1: Introduction to whole-genome sequencing and genome assembly.
Day 2: Assembly and gene-level analysis. Long-read sequencing.
Day 3: Core SNP and phylogenetic analysis.
Day 4: Metagenomics in pathogen detection.




This workshop is suitable for participants with little to no prior experience in bioinformatics. However, a basic understanding of clinical microbiology and molecular biology will be beneficial. No prior programming experience is required, as we will start from the fundamentals.



Each day is divided into several modules, each focusing on a specific topic. Each module includes:

Introduction: A brief overview of the topic.
Guided analyses: Practical tasks to analyse specific real-world datasets.
Code blocks: Commands and scripts to run the analyses.
Challenges: Optional advanced tasks to sharpen your skills.
Self exploration: Suggestions for further exploration of more advanced analysis techniques and tools.

We encourage you to try the challenges and self exploration sections. Be confident to ask questions and help each other. The goal is to learn by doing!
Let’s get started with Day 0!

title: 0.2 Commands & Environments
text:
Welcome to the command line! For many, the “black screen” of the terminal can be intimidating, but it is the most powerful tool in a bioinformatician’s toolkit. Most bioinformatics tools don’t have a graphical user interface (GUI) with buttons and menus; instead, they are controlled by typing commands.


You might wonder why we aren’t using Windows or macOS “Apps” for this. There are three main reasons:

Big Data: Genomic datasets are often too large for Excel or standard text editors. Linux tools are designed to stream data efficiently without crashing.
Automation: In genomics, we often need to run the same analysis on hundreds of samples. CLI (Command Line Interface) allows us to automate this with scripts.
Reproducibility: Typing commands creates a record of exactly what you did, making it easier to share and reproduce your research.




The Terminal (or console) is your window into the operating system. It provides a Shell, which is the program that interprets the commands you type and tells the computer what to do. On most modern systems, the default shell is bash or zsh.

The Prompt: Usually looks like user@computer:~$. The $ indicates that the shell is waiting for your input.
Case Sensitivity: Linux is case-sensitive. File.txt and file.txt are two different files.




You don’t need to memorize every command. Here is how to find out what a command does:

command --help: Most tools have a built-in help menu (e.g., ls --help).
man command: Opens the “manual” page (e.g., man ls). Press q to exit.
Search & AI: Google, Stack Overflow, and AI tools are your friends when you get stuck!




Think of the Linux file system as a giant tree. - Root (/): The base of the entire system. - Home Directory (~): Your personal space where you have full control. - Path: The “address” of a file or folder. - Absolute Path: Starts from the root (e.g., /home/user/data/sample.fastq). - Relative Path: Starts from where you currently are (e.g., data/sample.fastq).



A command usually follows this structure: command [options] [arguments]
Example: ls -l Documents/ - ls is the command (list files). - -l is an option (long format). - Documents/ is the argument (the folder to look into).



pwd (Print Working Directory): “Where am I?”
ls: “What’s in here?”

ls -F: Adds a / to directories.
ls -l: Shows detailed information (permissions, size, etc.).
ls -h: Human-readable sizes (KB, MB, GB).

cd: “Go somewhere else.”

cd ..: Move up one level.
cd ~: Go back home.

title: 0.2 Commands & Environments
text:
Anatomy of a File Name
In Windows, the file extension (.docx, .exe) tells the system what the file is. In Linux, extensions are mostly for humans. Linux determines what a file is by looking at its content (specifically “magic bytes” at the beginning), not its name.
You can name a file anything: - genome.fasta (Helpful) - lunch_list.banhmi (Valid) * data_source.nuocmam (Valid)
However, we usually keep standard extensions so we don’t confuse ourselves or our colleagues!

title: 0.2 Commands & Environments
text:
Types of Files in Genomics

Text Files: Plain text you can read. Examples: .fasta, .fastq, .csv, .sh.
Compressed Files: To save space, we often zip files. Common extensions: .gz, .zip, .tar.gz. Most Linux tools can “peek” inside .gz files without unzipping them.
Binary Files: Not human-readable. These are programs or optimized data formats (e.g., compiled tools, .bam or .bcf files).

title: 0.2 Commands & Environments
text:
Managing Files and Directories

mkdir: Create a new directory (which is just a special type of file!).

mkdir raw_reads

cp: Copy files or directories.

cp source.txt destination.txt

mv: Move or rename files.

mv old_name.txt new_name.txt

rm: Remove (delete) files. Be careful! There is no “Trash”.

rm -r folder_name: Recursively delete a directory.

title: 0.2 Commands & Environments
text:
Peeking into File Contents
We rarely open huge genomics files in editors. We use these instead:

cat: Print the whole file (only for small files!).
head: Show the first few lines.
tail: Show the last few lines.
less: The “gold standard” for viewing large files. Use arrows to scroll, / to search, and q to quit.

title: 0.2 Commands & Environments
text:
Searching and Filtering

grep: Search for a pattern (e.g., grep "sequence_ABC" all_sequences.fasta).
wc -l: Count the number of lines.

title: 0.2 Commands & Environments
text:
Access Permissions
Every file has metadata that defines who can do what with it. Run ls -l to see these.

r: Read
w: Write
x: Execute (run as a program)

These are divided into User (owner), Group, and Others.

title: 0.2 Commands & Environments
text:
chmod: Changing Permissions
To make a script executable: chmod +x my_analysis_script.sh

title: 0.2 Commands & Environments
text:
Pipes (|)
The pipe takes the output of one command and sends it as input to another. This allows you to build complex workflows by chaining simple tools.
Example: Count how many sequences are in a FASTA file. FASTA headers start with >. We can find all lines starting with > and count them: grep "^>" genome.fasta | wc -l

title: 0.2 Commands & Environments
text:
Standard Input, Output, and Error

Standard Out (stdout): Where the command sends its regular results (the screen).
Standard Error (stderr): Where the command sends error messages.
Redirection:

>: Save output to a file (overwrites).
>>: Append output to a file (adds to the end).
2>: Redirect error messages to a file.


Example: Save the list of files to a text file. ls -lh > file_list.txt

title: 0.2 Commands & Environments
text:
Variables
Variables store information for later use. In scripts, we often store filenames in variables. MY_SAMPLE="Sample_A1" echo $MY_SAMPLE

title: 0.2 Commands & Environments
section: Remote Computing: Connecting to a Server
text:
Remote Computing: Connecting to a Server
In bioinformatics, your laptop is often not powerful enough to process thousands of genomes. Instead, we use high-performance computers (HPC) or cloud servers. We connect to these using SSH (Secure Shell).
ssh username@server-ip-address
Once you are logged in, you are using the Linux environment of that remote computer!

title: 0.2 Commands & Environments
text:
Software Environments: Conda & Mamba
Different bioinformatics tools often require different versions of the same software. If you install everything together, they might clash. We use Conda (or the faster version, Mamba) to create isolated “bubbles” or environments.

mamba create -n assembly_env shovill spades: Create an environment for assembly.
mamba activate assembly_env: Enter the bubble.
mamba deactivate: Leave the bubble.

title: 0.2 Commands & Environments
text:
tmux & screen
Bioinformatics jobs often take hours or days to run. If your internet disconnects, the job might die. tmux allows you to run “sessions” that keep going even if you log out.

title: 0.2 Commands & Environments
text:
Loops
Automate tasks across hundreds of samples. for file in *.fastq; do echo "Processing $file..."; done

title: 0.2 Commands & Environments
text:
find
Find files based on name, size, or modification date. find . -name "*.vcf" -size +10M

title: 0.2 Commands & Environments
text:
GNU parallel
Run many commands at once using multiple CPU cores. A true game-changer for processing large cohorts.

title: Day 2: Inspect the genomes
text:
Following Day 1, on this day, we’re gonna learn how to analyse the genome assemblies to investigate the genomic features of the outbreak’s isolates, such as their multi-locus sequence type (MLST), antimicrobial resistance genes, virulence factors, and other genomic features.
We will journey through the following topics:

Assembly quality control: How to assess the quality of the de novo assemblies.
Species identification and MLST typing: How to identify the species and strain of bacteria.
AMR gene profiling: How to identify antimicrobial resistance genes.
Long-read sequencing & Hybrid assembly: How to use long-read sequencing data to improve genome assembly.
Genome annotation: How to annotate the genome with functional information.
Gene variation analysis: How to identify gene variations between isolates.

Now, let’s get started!



 Back to top

title: Beginner Course on Microbial Genomics:Applications & Research
text:
A bioinformatics workshop organised by the Molecular Epidemiology Group at Oxford University Clinical Research Unit in May, 2026.
This workshop is a part of the KLEB-CASAVA project, funded by the UKRI.
Aiming to enhance the local capacity in genomic epidemiology and surveillance, the workshop provides essential bioinformatics skills for Vietnamese clinicians and researchers, whose work focuses on bacterial infectious diseases, but have little to no experience in analysing whole-genome sequencing data.

title: 3.2 Phylogenetic tree inference
text: Back to top

title: 1.3 From raw sequencing data to assemblies
text:
From now until the end of Day-3, we will work with a real-world dataset: a nosocomial K. pneumoniae outbreak in Italy.
The dataset is under the BioProject ID: PRJNA1260529, acompanied by the publication of Captani et al, Euro Surveill, 2026. It describes an outbreak of multidrug-resistant and carbapenemase-producing K. pneumoniae ST147 in the sub-intensive care unit (S-ICU) of a tertiary care hospital in Italy between mid-February and early March 2025. NDM-producing K. pneumoniae isolates were sequenced using both Illumina short-read and Oxford Nanopore long-read technologies. The sequencing data helped illuminating the transmission dynamics of the outbreak and the genomic features of the isolates.
Using this dataset, we will learn how to transform “raw” sequencing data into “intepretable” genomes, and get a practical experience with a typical workflow in genomic epidemiology in the context of infectious disease outbreaks.
Our work-flow will comprise the following main processes:

Quality assessment (and improvement) of raw sequencing data
Genome assembly
Species identification and MLST typing
AMR gene profiling
Genome annotation
Gene variation analysis
Phylogenetic analysis

This module will guide you through the first 2 processes.
We hope you will enjoy the flow. Now, let’s begin!
crumbs: 1.3 From raw sequencing data to assemblies

title: 1.3 From raw sequencing data to assemblies
text:
MultiQC report
Now you can open the file multiqc_shortread/multiqc_report.html in your browser to view the report. Or in the VSCode, right click on the file and select “Open Preview”.
Let’s go through the report and answer the following questions:

What was the coverage?
What is the overall quality of the raw reads?
Are there any adapter contamination?
Are there any issues with the sequencing?







ImportantMaking decision





Based on the MultiQC report, do you want to improve the raw reads quality?
If yes, what specific modification to the raw reads would you like to perform? If no, then why?
crumbs: 1.3 From raw sequencing data to assemblies

title: 1.3 From raw sequencing data to assemblies
text:
Improve reads quality for assembly
Trimming and adapter removal can be done with FASTP or Trimmomatic
Example command-lines:
crumbs: 1.3 From raw sequencing data to assemblies

title: 1.3 From raw sequencing data to assemblies
section: Is my isolate really a K. pneumoniae?
text:
Is my isolate really a K. pneumoniae?
Species check can be done directly on the raw short-reads sequencing data, thanks to techniques from metagenomics.
SYLPH_DB="sylph-db/gtdb-r220-c200-dbv1.syldb"
sylph profile $SYLPH_DB -1 raw_shortread/*_1.fastq.gz -2 raw_shortread/*_2.fastq.gz -o sylph_shortread.tsv
crumbs: 1.3 From raw sequencing data to assemblies

title: Day 1: Meet the Genomes
text: Welcome to the first core day of our workshop! Having established our technical foundation with Linux in Day 0, we now transition into the heart of bacterial genomics. Today’s journey, “Meet the Genomes”, is designed to take you from a theoretical understanding of Whole-Genome Sequencing (WGS), to the practical reality of generating high-quality bacterial assemblies from raw data.

title: Day 1: Meet the Genomes
text:
1.1 Whole-genome sequencing - What & Why
We begin with the “what” and “why” of WGS. This module covers the fundamental concepts of sequencing technologies and their critical applications in: * Clinical Microbiology: Enhancing diagnostic speed and precision. * Outbreak Investigation: Inferring the transmission dynamics from the genomic relationships between the isolates. * AMR Surveillance: Identifying the genetic determinants of drug resistance. * Case Study Introduction: We will introduce the real-world Klebsiella pneumoniae dataset from a recent hospital outbreak that will serve as our primary study material.

title: Day 1: Meet the Genomes
text:
1.2 Studying Public Genomes
Genomic epidemiology relies heavily on comparative analysis. In this module, you will learn to navigate the vast archives of public genomic data: * NCBI Resources: Searching for and retrieving reference genomes. * Genome Visualization: Hands-on training with Artemis — a powerful tool for browsing through bacterial genomes, inspecting gene regions, and understanding genomic features.

title: Day 1: Meet the Genomes
text:
1.3 From Raw Data to Assembly
At the end of the day, we’ll put theory into practice. Using an K. pneumoniae outbreak dataset, you will learn the essential bioinformatic pipeline to process raw short-read sequencing data: * Quality Control (QC): Identifying issues in raw sequencing reads using FastQC and MultiQC. * De novo Assembly: Using Unicycler to reconstruct full genomes from short-read sequences.

title: Day 1: Meet the Genomes
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of today, you will be able to: 1. Explain the utility of WGS in clinical and public health microbiology. 2. Retrieve and visualize bacterial genomes from public databases. 3. Perform quality control on raw sequencing data. 4. Generate bacterial genome assemblies using state-of-the-art bioinformatic tools.

Let’s begin our journey into the bacterial genome!
